Top picks

Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built In Grill, Stainless Steel Grill Head for Outdoor Kitchen, Natural Gas

The Hygrill STD Series is aimed squarely at homeowners building a permanent outdoor kitchen who want a drop-in grill head with a flawless track record — it carries a perfect 5/5 rating across 41 verified reviews. Its standout strength is the combination of heavy-duty stainless steel construction and the no-compromise built-in format: there's no freestanding frame to corrode or wobble, just a solid grill head designed to sit flush in a custom island surround for years. If longevity and a clean, professional-grade installation matter more than burner count, this is a compelling choice at $1,129.99.

The trade-off is obvious: three burners limits the simultaneous cooking zones compared to four- or five-burner competitors at similar or lower price points. For a household that regularly feeds large groups or wants distinct temperature zones running at once, that constraint will be felt. It's also a dedicated natural gas unit in a built-in configuration, so it's not a flexible or portable option — this grill is a permanent commitment.

Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head, 30-Inch 4-Burner Propane Grill, Natural Gas Convertible, Heavy Duty 304 Stainless Steel 40,000BTUs BBQ Island Outdoor Kitchen

The Brand-Man 30-inch built-in head earns its spot through a combination of high verified satisfaction (5/5 across 72 reviews — the largest review pool of any built-in head on this list) and genuine fuel flexibility. The natural gas convertible design means it can be installed directly on a new NG line or run on propane in the interim, which matters a great deal during phased outdoor kitchen builds. Four burners and 40,000 BTUs give it enough output and zone control for most family-sized gatherings, all wrapped in 304-grade stainless steel that resists coastal and humid climates better than lower-grade alloys. At $889.99 it undercuts the Hygrill on price while adding a burner.

Where it falls short is in raw output relative to its price tier — 40,000 BTUs across four burners is a respectable but not exceptional figure, and buyers who want high-heat searing performance will find competitors with more BTU density. The 30-inch footprint also occupies meaningful island real estate without delivering the sheer cooking area that larger 4-burner configurations offer. Anyone planning a sprawling island should verify cutout dimensions carefully before purchasing.

Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle for Outdoor Kitchen Grill, Durable Stainless Steel 2-Burner Flat Top Grill with Lid for BBQ Island, 34,000 BTUs

This Stanbroil unit fills a specific gap that conventional grate grills cannot: a dedicated flat-top griddle surface built into a permanent outdoor kitchen. It's the right pick for cooks who want to do smash burgers, pancake breakfasts, stir-fry, or anything that benefits from a continuous cooking surface rather than grates. The 34,000 BTU output across two burners is well-suited to the flat-top format, where even heat distribution across the plate matters more than peak flame intensity. The included lid and stainless steel build make it a weather-resistant, permanent installation piece, and at $1,109.99 it's priced competitively for a purpose-built outdoor griddle. It holds a solid 4.5/5 across 20 reviews.

The limitation is its narrower appeal and smaller review base compared to grate-style competitors. Anyone expecting traditional grilled char marks or open-flame cooking will need to pair this with a separate grill — the flat top is a complement, not a replacement, in most setups. The 28-inch width and two-burner configuration also mean it's not suited for high-volume cooking of large cuts simultaneously. For a multi-cook outdoor kitchen, it works best as a secondary station alongside a full grill head rather than the centerpiece. See the Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen guide for pairing ideas.

Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Propane or Natural Gas Island Grill – 864 sq. in. Cooking, 48,000 BTU Main + 12,000 BTU Side Burner, Porcelain Grates, Storage Cabinets, Semi-Portable - GM/Ash Stone

The Tytus Fresno is the high-capacity choice for buyers who want an island grill station that doesn't require being cemented into a permanent structure. Its semi-portable design with built-in storage cabinets and an ash stone finish gives it the visual weight of a fixed outdoor kitchen while retaining the ability to reposition it — a meaningful advantage for renters, seasonal homes, or anyone not ready to commit to a permanent build. The cooking numbers back up the premium $1,399.99 price tag: 864 square inches of cooking area, 48,000 BTUs across five main burners, and a 12,000 BTU side burner give it the most raw capacity on this list. Dual-fuel compatibility with natural gas and propane adds further flexibility.

The trade-offs are a lower average rating (4.2/5 from 16 reviews — the fewest reviews here) and the inherent compromise of the semi-portable format. The storage cabinet structure, while attractive, won't match the structural rigidity or weather resistance of a fully built-in masonry or steel-frame island. Porcelain grates require more careful maintenance than cast stainless alternatives to avoid chipping over time. Given the limited review sample, there's less long-term owner data to draw on compared to the Brand-Man or Monument options on this page.

Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island with Side Burner - 44,000 BTU Stainless Steel BBQ Grill Station with Storage Cabinets, Fold-out Prep Table, LPG/NG Convertible, Black

This Brand-Man island station is the best all-in-one value on the list and the most purchased — 113 reviews at a 4.7/5 rating is a strong signal of consistent real-world satisfaction. At $799.99 it delivers a complete outdoor cooking station: four main burners, a side burner, fold-out prep table, storage cabinets, and LPG/NG convertibility, all in one package that requires no custom stonework or separate island construction. The black stainless finish gives it a modern look that holds up visually against patio furniture and deck surrounds. For a buyer who wants a capable, full-featured setup without the cost and lead time of a built-in kitchen, this is the most practical path. It's featured among the broader options at Natural Gas Outdoor Grills — Best Picks, Reviews & Guides.

The compromise is depth of construction relative to true built-in grill heads. A freestanding island cabinet, regardless of quality, is more vulnerable to long-term weather exposure than a drop-in head set into stone or steel framing. The 44,000 BTU rating is solid but spread across four burners plus a side burner, so per-zone output is moderate rather than high. Buyers planning a permanent, high-end outdoor kitchen will likely outgrow this format and wish they had invested in a built-in head from the start.

Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner Propane Gas Grills Stainless Steel Cabinet Style with Clearview® Lid, Knob Controls, Built-In Thermometer, Infrared Side Sear Burners & Side Burners, Blue

The Monument Grills 4+2 burner unit is the entry point for buyers who want meaningful searing capability without paying built-in prices. The inclusion of infrared side sear burners is the standout feature at this price — infrared technology delivers the kind of concentrated, intense heat needed for proper steakhouse-style crust formation, which most grills in the sub-$600 range simply don't offer. The Clearview lid, built-in thermometer, and cabinet-style base round out a spec sheet that punches above its $519 price point, and a 5/5 rating across 71 reviews confirms buyers are consistently satisfied. It's a strong pick for anyone who wants a freestanding natural gas setup with legitimate sear performance. Compare it against similar configurations at Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas and Best Barbecue Grills Natural Gas.

The trade-off is the freestanding cabinet format and the fact that this is a propane unit in its base configuration — natural gas conversion compatibility should be confirmed before purchase for buyers on an NG line. It also lacks the integrated prep table and storage versatility of the Brand-Man island station despite sitting at a similar conceptual tier. The blue color option is distinctive but limits how well it blends into neutral-toned patio setups. Buyers prioritizing a seamless aesthetic or a built-in island appearance will need to look elsewhere, but for pure cooking performance per dollar, it's hard to fault. See also Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas for additional comparisons.

Buying guide

BTU output is the most cited spec in this category, but raw numbers only matter in context. A 3-burner built-in like the Hygrill STD Series covers a compact cooking zone, so its BTU rating does more work per square inch than the 48,000 BTU main burner spread across the Tytus Fresno's 864 sq. in. cooking surface. As a rule, look for at least 10,000–12,000 BTU per burner on a natural gas setup, since NG pressure runs slightly lower than propane at the same regulator setting. The Brand-Man 4-Burner head hits 40,000 BTU across four burners — roughly 10,000 BTU each — which sits right at that floor. If you're cooking for large groups regularly, the Tytus Fresno's 60,000 combined BTU (main plus side burner) gives meaningful headroom.

Material grade separates grills that survive a decade of outdoor exposure from ones that pit and rust within a few seasons. The products here split between generic "stainless steel" and specified 304-grade stainless. 304 is the meaningful designation — it contains roughly 18% chromium and 8% nickel, making it genuinely corrosion-resistant rather than just shiny. The Brand-Man 30-inch built-in head explicitly calls out 304 stainless, and the Stanbroil griddle specifies "durable stainless steel" with a lid included to protect the flat-top surface. If a listing doesn't specify the alloy grade, that's worth asking about before committing $800 or more. Grate material matters too: porcelain-coated cast iron, as used on the Tytus Fresno, retains heat well and is easier to season than bare steel, but chips if handled roughly.

Decide early whether you want a drop-in grill head or a freestanding island unit — the two formats serve different builds. Drop-in heads like the Hygrill STD Series, the Stanbroil griddle, and the Brand-Man 30-inch are designed to be cut into a masonry or prefabricated outdoor kitchen counter. They look cleaner but require an existing structure. Freestanding island grills like the Brand-Man 4-Burner station and the Tytus Fresno include their own storage cabinets and, in the Brand-Man's case, a fold-out prep table — useful if you're building from scratch or need portability. The Monument Grills 4+2 burner is a traditional cart-style grill with a cabinet base, infrared side sear burners, and a ClearView lid, making it the most practical choice if you want a plug-and-play freestanding unit without committing to a full outdoor kitchen build. For more built-in options, see Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026).

Natural gas convertibility is a practical filter if your setup uses NG from a fixed line. Several models here — the Brand-Man 30-inch head and the Brand-Man island station — are listed as LPG/NG convertible, meaning they ship with or support a conversion kit. The Tytus Fresno is explicitly offered in a propane-or-natural-gas configuration. Confirm that any conversion kit is included rather than sold separately, and verify the orifice size matches your local gas supply pressure before installation. If you're comparing across the broader category, Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas (2026) and Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas (2026) cover additional formats and price points.

Related guides

If you're building out a full outdoor kitchen setup, the best gas grills for outdoor kitchens guide covers built-in options across a wider range of configurations and budgets. For a broader look at the natural gas category including freestanding and cart-style models, the best barbeque grills for natural gas roundup is worth a read alongside this one.

Frequently asked questions

What is the minimum budget for a heavy-duty natural gas grill?

Expect to spend at least $500 for a reputable heavy-duty natural gas grill. The Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner model comes in at $519 and represents the lower end of the range on this page. Built-in grill heads and full island stations with 304 stainless steel construction typically start around $800–$900, with premium options like the Hygrill STD Series and Stanbroil 28-Inch Griddle pushing past $1,100. A full island setup with storage cabinets, such as the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner, reaches $1,399.99. Buying below the $500 mark on a natural gas unit usually means thinner steel, weaker burners, and shorter service life.

Can I convert a propane grill to natural gas?

Several models on this page are designed for dual-fuel flexibility. The Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner Built-In Head is listed as "Natural Gas Convertible," and the Brand-Man 4-Burner Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island is explicitly rated for both LPG and NG. The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner also supports both propane and natural gas. Conversion typically requires a manufacturer-supplied kit with different orifices sized for the lower pressure of a natural gas line. Never swap fuels without the correct kit — mismatched orifices create dangerous flame conditions and will void most warranties.

What is the difference between a built-in grill head and a freestanding grill?

A built-in grill head — like the Hygrill STD Series, the Stanbroil 28-Inch Griddle, or the Brand-Man 30-Inch Head — is a drop-in unit designed to sit inside a masonry or prefabricated outdoor kitchen island. It has no legs and no side shelves of its own; the surrounding structure provides all support and counter space. A freestanding grill stands on its own frame and can be repositioned. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island and Tytus Fresno are self-contained island stations that include cabinets, side burners, and prep surfaces in one unit. If you are building a permanent outdoor kitchen, a built-in head gives the cleanest look; if you want flexibility or do not have an existing island, a freestanding island station is the practical choice. For more on integrating a grill into an outdoor kitchen, see Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026).

How many BTUs do I actually need?

BTU ratings matter less than burner count and cooking surface efficiency. For context, the models here range from 34,000 BTUs on the Stanbroil 2-burner griddle to 48,000 BTUs on the Tytus Fresno's main burners (plus a 12,000 BTU side burner). A good rule of thumb is roughly 80–100 BTUs per square inch of primary cooking area. More important is whether the burners distribute heat evenly across the grate — high total BTUs with poor distribution still produce hot and cold spots. If searing is the priority, look for infrared burner options; the Monument Grills model includes infrared side sear burners alongside its 4+2 burner layout.

Which brands on this page have the strongest owner ratings?

Based on verified review counts provided, the Hygrill STD Series and Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner Built-In Head both hold a 5/5 rating — the Hygrill from 41 reviews and the Brand-Man from 72. The Monument Grills 4+2 Burner also carries 5/5 across 71 reviews. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island has the highest review volume at 113, with a 4.7/5 score. The Stanbroil Griddle sits at 4.5/5 from 20 reviews, and the Tytus Fresno at 4.2/5 from 16 — both newer to market with smaller sample sizes. For a broader comparison of natural gas options across categories, see the Natural Gas Outdoor Grills hub.

Is a flat-top griddle a practical alternative to a traditional grate grill for natural gas outdoor cooking?

Yes, particularly if you cook for larger groups or frequently prepare foods that fall through standard grates — vegetables, fish, smash burgers, breakfast items. The Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle offers 34,000 BTUs across two burners and a flat stainless steel cooking surface with a lid, designed to drop into an outdoor kitchen island just like a conventional grill head. The trade-off is that you lose the open-flame char you get from grates. Many serious outdoor kitchens include both a grate grill head and a griddle side by side. For additional natural gas barbecue grill options, the linked guide covers a wider selection.

Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built In Grill, Stainless Steel Grill Head for Outdoor Kitchen, Natural Gas

The trimmed-down 3-burner layout keeps the footprint compact without sacrificing the output needed for serious barbecue sessions, making it a smart fit for modestly sized kitchen islands where a sprawling 4- or 5-burner head would be overkill.

At $1,129.99 it sits at the higher end for a 3-burner configuration, and because it's a grill head only, buyers still need to budget for a surrounding island structure. Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head, 30-Inch 4-Burner Propane Grill, Natural Gas Convertible, Heavy Duty 304 Stainless Steel 40,000BTUs BBQ Island Outdoor Kitchen

The Brand-Man 30-inch built-in head punches well above its $889.99 price point, delivering 40,000 BTUs across four burners through a 304 stainless steel body — the same food-grade alloy found in commercial kitchens. The natural gas convertibility means it isn't locked into one fuel source, a practical advantage if you're tying it into an existing gas line but want flexibility for the future. With a 5/5 average across 72 reviews, it has the deepest approval rating of any built-in head on this page, which builds genuine purchase confidence. Porcelain cooking grates retain heat evenly and resist corrosion better than plain cast iron, which matters for natural gas grills used frequently through all seasons. With only 16 reviews at 4.2/5, the Tytus has the thinnest owner feedback record on this page — a meaningful caveat when spending nearly $1,400. The Stanbroil flat-top griddle fills a gap that traditional grate grills can't — a smooth cooking surface ideal for smash burgers, breakfast foods, seafood, and vegetables that would fall through conventional grates. At 34,000 BTUs across two burners and a 28-inch built-in format, it's designed to pair with an existing outdoor kitchen island, either as a standalone station or alongside a separate grill head. The included lid protects the flat top when not in use, which extends surface life significantly. A 4.5/5 average from 20 reviews is solid for a specialized piece of equipment that occupies a more niche role than a full multi-burner grill.

This is emphatically not a direct substitute for a conventional barbecue grill — there are no grates, no open flame cooking, and no sear marks. Buyers who want both flat-top and grate cooking need to budget for two separate units or choose a hybrid. At $1,109.99 for a 2-burner, 28-inch unit, the price per BTU is high compared to the multi-burner options, which is typical for specialized griddle inserts. Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner Propane Gas Grills Stainless Steel Cabinet Style with Clearview® Lid, Knob Controls, Built-In Thermometer, Infrared Side Sear Burners & Side Burners, Blue

Monument's cabinet-style unit is the accessibility pick — $519 for a 4+2 burner configuration that includes infrared side sear burners, two side burners, a built-in thermometer, and the distinctive Clearview® lid that lets you monitor food without lifting and losing heat. That feature set at that price is genuinely unusual. A 5/5 average across 71 reviews gives it strong credibility for budget-conscious buyers who don't want to compromise on cooking versatility. The listing specifies propane, not natural gas, as the primary fuel — conversion compatibility for natural gas is not confirmed in the available product data, so buyers who need direct natural gas connection should verify this before purchasing. As a freestanding cabinet-style grill rather than a built-in or true island unit, it won't integrate into a permanent outdoor kitchen structure. Build quality at the $519 price point will naturally trail the heavier-gauge stainless found in units costing twice as much, which is worth factoring in if this grill will live outdoors year-round in a harsh climate. See the Best Grills For Natural Gas (2026) guide for additional options if natural gas compatibility is a firm requirement.

Buying guide

BTU output and cooking area are the two specs buyers anchor on, and for good reason — they determine whether a grill can run hot enough to sear properly and accommodate a full cook. The products here range from 34,000 BTUs across two burners on the Stanbroil 28-Inch Built-In Natural Gas Griddle to 48,000 BTUs across five main burners on the Tytus Fresno Island Grill, with a dedicated 12,000 BTU side burner on top of that. As a rule of thumb, heavy-duty outdoor grills should deliver at least 80–100 BTUs per square inch of cooking surface to maintain consistent high heat. The Tytus Fresno's 864 sq. in. of cooking space paired with its BTU figure clears that bar comfortably. Anything noticeably below that ratio will struggle at peak capacity. Best Gas Grills Natural Gas (2026).

Construction grade separates grills that last a season from ones that last a decade. Look specifically for 304 stainless steel — it's the alloy that resists corrosion and pitting in outdoor environments. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Built-In Head and the Brand-Man Grill Island both call out 304 stainless explicitly, and the Stanbroil griddle is marketed as premium stainless. Grate material matters too: porcelain-coated cast iron grates, as on the Tytus Fresno, hold heat evenly and release food cleanly, while straight stainless rod grates are durable but require more active temperature management. For a built-in application — whether a full BBQ island or a drop-in head — frame rigidity and weather resistance are non-negotiable, since the unit won't be moved under cover. If you're building a full outdoor kitchen setup, the best gas grills for outdoor kitchen guide covers integration considerations in more depth.

Fuel flexibility and installation type are practical decisions that need to be made before purchase, not after. Several options here — including the Brand-Man Built-In Head and the Tytus Fresno — ship as propane units with natural gas conversion included, which keeps your options open if your outdoor setup isn't yet plumbed. The Hygrill STD Series and Stanbroil griddle are native natural gas units, so verify your gas line pressure and regulator compatibility before ordering either. Built-in heads (the Hygrill, Brand-Man head, Stanbroil) require a prepared cutout in a countertop or island; freestanding island units like the Brand-Man Grill Station and Tytus Fresno arrive as self-contained structures with storage cabinets and prep surfaces built in — the Brand-Man station includes fold-out prep table and storage cabinets, the Tytus adds storage cabinets as well.

Feature additions like side burners, infrared sear zones, and storage make a real difference depending on how you cook. The Monument Grills 4+2 Burner is the only option here with dedicated infrared side sear burners alongside conventional side burners, which is valuable for finishing steaks at extreme heat without a separate appliance. A clear-view lid — also on the Monument — lets you monitor food without lifting the hood and bleeding heat. If flat-top griddle cooking is the goal rather than open-flame grilling, the Stanbroil is the only dedicated flat-top in this group, with a full 28-inch stainless cooking surface suited to smash burgers, eggs, and fish that would fall through traditional grates. Match the feature set to how you actually cook rather than defaulting to the highest burner count. For a broader look across price points and configurations, see the natural gas outdoor grills hub or compare options in the best barbeque grills natural gas roundup.

Related guides

If you're building out a full outdoor kitchen setup, the guide to best gas grills for outdoor kitchen covers built-in heads and drop-in options suited for permanent installations. For a broader look at the category before narrowing down, the natural gas outdoor grills hub pulls together top picks, reviews, and buying guides across every style and budget.

Frequently asked questions

What is the price floor for a heavy-duty natural gas grill worth buying?

Expect to spend at least $500 for a grill with genuine heavy-duty construction. The Monument Grills 4+2 Burner model sits at $519 and represents roughly the entry point — it delivers a cabinet-style frame, infrared side sear burners, and a clear-view lid at that price. Purpose-built island grill heads and full grill stations from brands like Brand-Man, Hygrill, and Tytus run $800–$1,400 depending on burner count, BTU output, and included storage. Anything well below $500 that claims "heavy duty" is almost always a compromise on steel gauge or burner quality.

How do I convert a propane grill to natural gas?

Many grills on this page are factory-rated as LPG/NG convertible — the Brand-Man 30-inch built-in head and the Brand-Man 4-burner island station are both explicitly listed as natural gas convertible. Conversion typically requires swapping the orifices (jets) at each burner and, in some cases, adjusting the regulator. The conversion kit is sometimes included, sometimes sold separately. Always verify with the manufacturer before purchasing, and have a licensed gas technician make the connection to your natural gas line. Do not attempt to run a propane-only grill on natural gas with improvised parts.

Built-in grill head vs. full grill station — which should I choose?

A built-in grill head like the Hygrill STD Series or the Brand-Man 30-inch head drops into a custom outdoor kitchen island you already own or are having built. That route gives you full control over counter material, cabinet layout, and overall footprint. A full grill station like the Brand-Man 4-burner island or the Tytus Fresno comes with storage cabinets, prep tables, and sometimes a side burner already integrated — you set it up and you're done. If you're building an outdoor kitchen from scratch and want flexibility, go the built-in route. If you want a self-contained outdoor cooking station without construction work, a full station is faster and often more cost-effective. See Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026) for a deeper comparison.

How many BTUs do I actually need for a backyard natural gas grill?

BTU ratings are frequently misused as a quality signal — raw output matters less than burner-to-cooking-area ratio and grate material. As a rough guide, 80–100 BTUs per square inch of primary cooking surface is a reasonable target for high-heat searing. The Tytus Fresno delivers 48,000 BTUs across a 60,000+ BTU total setup with a dedicated side burner, which is more than enough for large gatherings. The Brand-Man 30-inch built-in head provides 40,000 BTUs across four burners. Chasing the highest BTU number without considering cooking area, lid design, and grate heat retention is a common buyer mistake.

What return policies should I expect when buying a grill at this price point?

Return policies vary significantly by retailer, not just brand. At the $800–$1,400 range, most major online retailers offer 30-day return windows, but grills that have been assembled or used are often non-returnable or subject to restocking fees. Built-in grill heads are especially difficult to return once installed. Before purchasing, confirm the seller's policy on opened or assembled items, and check whether freight shipping damage is covered. Warranty terms on the grill itself — typically one to several years on burners and construction — are separate from the purchase return window.

Is a flat-top griddle a better fit than a traditional grate grill for natural gas outdoor cooking?

It depends entirely on what you cook most. A flat-top like the Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle gives you an even cooking surface that excels at smash burgers, breakfast items, stir-fry, and anything that benefits from full contact with heat. Traditional grate grills produce direct-flame char and grill marks that a griddle cannot replicate. Many serious outdoor kitchens include both — a grate grill as the main unit and a griddle insert or side station for versatility. The Stanbroil's 34,000 BTU output across two burners and its stainless lid make it a practical complement to a primary grill rather than a replacement for one. Browse Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas (2026) for more configuration ideas.

At $1,109.99 it's priced comparably to the Hygrill grill head, which is a meaningful cost to absorb for a griddle-only unit. It won't sear with grill marks or produce the char flavor that a grate grill delivers, so it's not a substitute — it's a complement. Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner Propane Gas Grills Stainless Steel Cabinet Style with Clearview® Lid, Knob Controls, Built-In Thermometer, Infrared Side Sear Burners & Side Burners, Blue

Monument Grills delivers the most feature-packed option under $600 in this group. The "Blue" colorway and cabinet-style design read as more consumer-grade than the stainless island aesthetics of the higher-end options here — fine for most backyards, but not a match for a high-end outdoor kitchen build. Buying guide

BTU output sets the ceiling on how hot a grill can run, but raw numbers only matter in context. The Tytus Fresno delivers 48,000 BTU across five main burners plus a dedicated 12,000 BTU side burner, which makes sense for a large cooking island where you need distinct heat zones. The Brand-Man 4-burner built-in head puts out 40,000 BTU across four burners, and the freestanding Brand-Man island station reaches 44,000 BTU with a side burner included. For most households, anything in the 34,000–48,000 BTU range is sufficient — the more important question is how that output is distributed across burners, since uneven zone control is what causes hot spots and frustration during long cooks.

Cooking surface and grate material determine practical capacity and long-term durability. The Tytus Fresno stands out with 864 sq. in. of total cooking area and porcelain-coated grates, which retain heat well and resist rust better than bare cast iron if maintenance is inconsistent. The Stanbroil griddle takes a different approach entirely — it's a 28-inch flat-top design with a 34,000 BTU two-burner layout, suited specifically to smash burgers, breakfast foods, and anything that benefits from a solid cooking surface rather than open grates. If you're building an outdoor kitchen setup, matching the grill format — built-in head versus full island station — to your existing cabinetry is as important as the BTU spec.

Construction grade separates grills that last a decade from those that corrode within a few seasons. The Hygrill STD Series and Brand-Man built-in head both specify 304 stainless steel, which is the food-grade alloy that resists chloride corrosion from coastal air and humidity. The Stanbroil griddle also uses stainless steel construction. Thinner 430-grade stainless is common at lower price points and will show surface rust within a couple of years in humid climates. The Monument Grills 4+2 burner unit is the most affordable option here at $519 and adds infrared side sear burners plus a ClearView lid — useful features, but the spec sheet doesn't confirm 304-grade steel, which is worth verifying before buying for a permanent outdoor installation.

Natural gas compatibility is non-negotiable for a fixed outdoor setup since you can't swap tanks. Several options here — including the Brand-Man built-in head and the Brand-Man island station — are listed as LPG/NG convertible, meaning they ship with conversion hardware or can be ordered in the correct configuration. The Tytus Fresno and Stanbroil griddle are sold directly as natural gas units. Confirm the exact SKU before purchasing, because some retailers list both fuel variants under the same product page. Frequently asked questions

What is the price floor for a quality natural gas barbecue grill?

Expect to spend at least $500–$550 for a full-featured freestanding natural gas grill. The Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner model sits at $519 and represents the realistic entry point for a heavy-duty stainless steel cabinet-style unit with infrared sear burners. Built-in grill heads for outdoor kitchens start closer to $890, as seen with the Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner Built-In Head at $889.99. Island systems with storage cabinets and side burners run $800–$1,400 depending on burner count and BTU output. barbecue grills natural gas (2026).

Should I buy a built-in grill head or a freestanding island system?

A built-in grill head — like the Hygrill STD Series 3-Burner or the Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner — drops into a custom masonry or prefab frame you already own or plan to build. It gives you flexibility over counter material and cabinet layout but requires separate infrastructure. A freestanding island system such as the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island or the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island arrives as a complete unit with storage cabinets and a prep table, which is faster to set up and typically costs less overall once you factor out custom construction. If you already have an outdoor kitchen frame, go built-in. If you are starting from scratch, an island system usually delivers better value. See Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026) for a deeper comparison.

Can propane grills be converted to natural gas?

Several models on this page are sold as convertible. The Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner Built-In Head is explicitly listed as "Natural Gas Convertible," and the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island is noted as "LPG/NG Convertible." The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island also ships ready for either propane or natural gas. Conversion typically requires a different orifice kit; confirm the kit is included before purchasing, and always have a licensed gas technician handle the connection to your natural gas line.

How do BTU ratings compare across these grills, and does higher BTU mean better grilling?

BTU output across this page ranges from 34,000 BTU (Stanbroil 28-Inch 2-Burner Griddle) to 60,000 BTU combined on the Tytus Fresno (48,000 BTU main + 12,000 BTU side burner). The Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner head delivers 40,000 BTU, while the Brand-Man Island reaches 44,000 BTU. Higher BTU matters for heat recovery time and high-heat searing, but cooking surface area and grate material matter equally. The Tytus Fresno's 864 sq. in. cooking area, for example, means its 48,000 BTU main burner is spread across more surface than a compact 3-burner head. BTU-per-square-inch is a more useful metric than raw BTU alone.

What should I look for in grates on a natural gas barbecue grill?

Cast iron retains heat best for sear marks but requires seasoning and dries out if left exposed. Porcelain-coated cast iron — used on the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner — reduces maintenance while keeping heat retention. Stainless steel grates found on most 304-grade models are corrosion-resistant and easier to clean but release heat faster. For a built-in that stays outdoors year-round, porcelain-coated or heavy-gauge stainless is the more practical choice.

Are these natural gas grills suitable for permanent outdoor kitchen installations?

The built-in heads — Hygrill STD Series, Brand-Man 30-Inch, and Stanbroil 28-Inch Griddle — are specifically designed for permanent drop-in installation in BBQ islands. All three use 304 stainless steel construction, which resists corrosion in outdoor environments. The Stanbroil griddle adds a lid, which helps protect the flat-top surface when not in use. For a full guide to permanent setups, visit Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026) or browse the broader Natural Gas Outdoor Grills hub for additional options by format and budget.

At $799.99 it includes storage cabinets, a fold-out prep table, a side burner, and the main 4-burner grill producing 44,000 BTUs — LPG/NG convertible, so it works on natural gas straight from the line. The 4.7/5 rating across 113 reviews is the largest review base on this list, which gives that score more statistical weight than perfect scores from smaller samples. The black finish and integrated cabinet design give it a cohesive look that freestanding grills on carts rarely match. gas grills natural gas (2026).

The integrated design also limits flexibility — if the grill head eventually needs replacement, you're not swapping it independently. The black finish, while sharp-looking, requires more maintenance in harsh climates than brushed stainless exteriors. It also won't satisfy buyers looking for a purely built-in flush installation; this is a station, not a drop-in head. For comparable island options in the natural gas outdoor grill category, it's hard to beat at this price.

Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Propane or Natural Gas Island Grill – 864 sq. in. Cooking, 48,000 BTU Main + 12,000 BTU Side Burner, Porcelain Grates, Storage Cabinets, Semi-Portable - GM/Ash Stone

The Tytus Fresno targets serious outdoor entertainers who want maximum cooking real estate and output in a single unit. With 864 square inches of cooking surface, 48,000 BTUs across five main burners, an additional 12,000 BTU side burner, storage cabinets, and porcelain grates, it covers virtually every outdoor cooking scenario without requiring anything extra. The semi-portable design is a genuine differentiator — it can be repositioned, which matters for renters or anyone who may not want a permanently fixed installation. At $1,399.99 it's the highest-priced unit on this list.

The rating of 4.2/5 from only 16 reviews is the thinnest feedback base here, which introduces more uncertainty about long-term reliability. Semi-portability also means it lacks the permanence and flush integration of a true built-in, which some buyers specifically want. The ash stone and GM colorway is distinctive but not universally suited to every outdoor aesthetic. Those prioritizing built-in aesthetics for a kitchen island should weigh this against the Hygrill or Brand-Man head units covered above.

Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle for Outdoor Kitchen Grill, Durable Stainless Steel 2-Burner Flat Top Grill with Lid for BBQ Island, 34,000 BTUs

This is the specialist pick for cooks who prioritize flat-top versatility over traditional grate grilling. A 28-inch built-in griddle with 34,000 BTUs across two burners handles smash burgers, breakfast foods, stir-fry, seafood, and anything else that benefits from uniform surface contact rather than open flame. The stainless steel construction and lid make it a legitimate permanent outdoor kitchen component, not a portable tabletop afterthought. At $1,109.99 with a 4.5/5 rating from 20 reviews, it's priced appropriately for a purpose-built natural gas griddle head.

The limitation is fundamental to the format: it does not replace a traditional grill. Sear marks, smoke-kissed flavor, and rotisserie cooking aren't part of what a flat-top delivers. Buyers expecting a griddle to do everything a grill does will be disappointed. It works best as a companion piece to a grill head in a multi-station outdoor kitchen, not as a standalone solution. If you're evaluating the broader natural gas barbeque grill landscape, a griddle-only setup should be a deliberate choice.

Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner Propane Gas Grills Stainless Steel Cabinet Style with Clearview® Lid, Knob Controls, Built-In Thermometer, Infrared Side Sear Burners & Side Burners, Blue

Monument's cabinet-style grill is the practical pick for buyers who want a high-feature freestanding unit at an accessible price. At $519 with a 5/5 rating across 71 reviews, it punches above its price point with infrared side sear burners, two standard side burners, a Clearview lid, a built-in thermometer, and knob controls — all in a stainless cabinet body. The infrared sear burners are a meaningful upgrade at this price tier, enabling steakhouse-level searing that typically requires a much larger investment. It's listed as propane with no NG conversion noted, making it relevant for buyers on natural gas grill setups only if a conversion kit is separately available.

The natural gas compatibility question is the central caveat here. Unlike most other units on this list, there's no explicit NG convertibility stated in the product title or data — buyers on a natural gas line need to verify conversion availability before purchasing. It's also a freestanding cart unit rather than a built-in, which limits its appeal for permanent outdoor kitchen installations. Those building an integrated kitchen should look at the built-in head options; Monument is the right call for buyers who want a standalone, full-featured grill at a competitive price point.

Buying guide

BTU output is the first number to scrutinize, but raw heat means little without context. The products here range from a 34,000 BTU 2-burner flat-top griddle (the Stanbroil 28-Inch) up to the Tytus Fresno's 48,000 BTU main burner plus a dedicated 12,000 BTU side burner. More burners and higher total BTUs let you run distinct heat zones simultaneously — essential if you're searing steaks on one side while holding vegetables at a lower temperature on the other. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Island delivers 44,000 BTUs across four burners with an additional side burner, which gives meaningful zone control at a sub-$800 price. As a rough benchmark, look for at least 10,000 BTUs per burner to maintain reliable searing temperatures on a natural gas supply.

Cooking area and configuration split this category into two distinct paths: built-in grill heads designed to drop into an existing island, and freestanding island stations that arrive as complete units. The Hygrill STD Series and Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head are pure grill heads, meant for buyers who already have — or are planning — a custom outdoor kitchen structure. If you want everything in one purchase, the Brand-Man 4-Burner Island includes storage cabinets and a fold-out prep table, while the Tytus Fresno offers 864 sq. in. of total cooking surface with built-in storage and a semi-portable design. Matching the grill format to your existing setup avoids costly workarounds later. For more on fitting a grill into a full outdoor kitchen build, see Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026).

Material quality directly determines longevity in an outdoor environment. All the grills here use stainless steel in some form, but the grade matters. The Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head explicitly specifies 304 stainless steel, which offers superior corrosion resistance compared to the 430-grade steel commonly found in budget units. Grate material is equally important: porcelain-coated grates (as on the Tytus Fresno) retain heat well and resist sticking but can chip over time, while bare stainless grates are more durable but require more active seasoning. If you cook on high heat frequently and want low maintenance, stainless grates are the more durable long-term choice.

Fuel flexibility and convertibility is a practical concern worth checking before you buy. Several options here — including the Brand-Man Built-In Head and the Brand-Man Island — are sold as natural gas convertible, meaning they ship with or support a conversion kit for switching between propane and natural gas. The Tytus Fresno supports both propane and natural gas as well. If your outdoor space already has a natural gas line, a dedicated NG model is cleaner and more cost-effective to run, but a convertible unit gives you a fallback if you ever change locations. Always confirm the conversion kit is included or available before purchasing, since some manufacturers charge separately. For a broader look at compatible options, see Best Grills For Natural Gas (2026) and the full Natural Gas Outdoor Grills guide.

If you're outfitting a full outdoor kitchen, the guide to best gas grills for outdoor kitchen covers built-in heads and island-ready units in greater depth. For a broader look at the category, the best barbeque grills natural gas guide compares additional options across a wider price range and BTU spectrum.

Frequently asked questions

What is the minimum budget for a heavy-duty natural gas BBQ grill?

Entry-level heavy-duty natural gas grills start around $500–$520 for freestanding cabinet-style units like the Monument Grills 4+2 Burner. Built-in grill heads designed for outdoor kitchen islands typically begin closer to $800–$900, with fully outfitted island stations running $1,100–$1,400 and up. Plan to spend more if you want 304 stainless steel construction, high BTU output, or a complete station with storage and side burners included. Best Grills With Natural Gas (2026).

What is the difference between a built-in grill head and a freestanding grill island?

A built-in grill head — like the Hygrill STD Series or Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner — is just the cooking unit, dropped into a custom or prefabricated outdoor kitchen frame. You supply the surround, countertop, and cabinetry separately. A freestanding grill island, such as the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Station or the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner, arrives as a self-contained unit with the grill, storage cabinets, and prep surfaces already integrated. Built-in heads give more design flexibility; islands offer faster setup and a lower total cost if you don't already have an outdoor kitchen structure in place.

Can these natural gas grills be converted to propane, or vice versa?

Several models on this list are dual-fuel convertible. The Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner is listed as "Natural Gas Convertible," and the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Station and Tytus Fresno 5-Burner are explicitly LPG/NG convertible. Conversion typically requires a separate orifice kit and, in some cases, a regulator change — consult the manufacturer's instructions before switching fuels. Models sold as natural gas only, such as the Hygrill STD Series and the Stanbroil 28-Inch Griddle, should not be assumed convertible unless the product documentation confirms it.

How do I size a natural gas grill for the cooking space I need?

A rough rule of thumb is 72–80 sq. in. of primary cooking area per person you regularly cook for. For a household of four to six, 400–600 sq. in. is adequate for most sessions. The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner delivers 864 sq. in., which suits larger gatherings or serious outdoor entertainers. For a dedicated outdoor kitchen, a built-in griddle like the Stanbroil 28-Inch can complement a grill head rather than replace it, adding flat-top versatility without requiring a second island.

Is stainless steel grade important when comparing outdoor gas grills?

Yes. 304 stainless steel is the standard benchmark for heavy-duty outdoor grills — it offers better corrosion resistance than the 430-grade steel found in lower-cost units, which matters significantly in humid or coastal environments. The Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner explicitly calls out 304 stainless steel construction. When a listing simply says "stainless steel" without specifying grade, it is worth contacting the manufacturer to confirm before buying, especially for built-in installations where replacement is more disruptive.

Where can I find more guidance on choosing natural gas grills for an outdoor kitchen setup?

For built-in and island configurations specifically, see Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026). A broader look at the category is available at the Natural Gas Outdoor Grills hub, and side-by-side comparisons across more models are covered in Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas (2026) and Best Grills For Natural Gas (2026).

Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island with Side Burner - 44,000 BTU Stainless Steel BBQ Grill Station with Storage Cabinets, Fold-out Prep Table, LPG/NG Convertible, Black

The Stanbroil griddle occupies a distinct niche: it is the only flat-top option in this group, making it the right pick for outdoor kitchen builders who want to cook smash burgers, breakfast foods, stir-fry, or anything that benefits from a continuous cooking surface rather than grates. At 34,000 BTUs across two burners and a dedicated natural gas connection, it is engineered specifically for NG without conversion fuss. The stainless steel lid protects the flat top between sessions, an important feature for a surface that needs to stay seasoned and rust-free. It earns a 4.5/5 from 20 reviews — a respectable score for a specialized appliance. At $1,109.99 it is priced as a premium accessory, not a primary grill — and that framing matters. It does not replace a grate-style burner for traditional BBQ, so most buyers will treat this as a second drop-in unit alongside a conventional grill head. Budget-conscious builders who need to choose just one appliance should weigh whether the griddle format covers enough of their cooking needs to justify the cost.

Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner Propane Gas Grills Stainless Steel Cabinet Style with Clearview® Lid, Knob Controls, Built-In Thermometer, Infrared Side Sear Burners & Side Burners, Blue

The Monument Grills cabinet-style unit is the pick for buyers who want a freestanding grill with serious feature density at a price well below the rest of the lineup. At $519 it includes infrared side sear burners, two side burners, a Clearview® lid with built-in thermometer, and knob controls — a specification list that punches well above its price bracket. The 5/5 rating from 71 reviews is impressive for a value-tier product and suggests consistent buyer satisfaction. It suits budget-minded buyers or those who prefer a standalone freestanding grill over a built-in island format.

The primary limitation is that it ships as a propane unit — natural gas use requires a conversion, and the listing notes propane as the base fuel. The Clearview lid, while a useful feature, introduces a potential long-term durability variable compared to solid stainless lids used on higher-end units.

Buying guide

BTU output and cooking area are the two numbers that actually determine whether a natural gas grill can handle a serious cook. The products here range from 34,000 BTUs on the Stanbroil 28-Inch Built-In Griddle up to a combined 60,000 BTUs (48,000 main burners plus a 12,000 BTU side burner) on the Tytus Fresno. More BTUs matter most when you're searing large cuts or feeding a crowd quickly, but raw output only translates to performance when the cooking surface is sized to match — the Tytus backs its BTU count with 864 sq. in. of cooking area, while the Stanbroil's flat-top format concentrates heat differently than a traditional grate setup. As a rule, budget around 80–100 BTUs per square inch of primary cooking surface for adequate heat across the whole grill.

Construction grade separates grills that last a decade from ones that corrode in two seasons. The most durable builds here use 304 stainless steel — a marine-grade alloy with significantly better corrosion resistance than the 430 stainless common in lower-cost units. The Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head and the Hygrill STD Series both specify 304 stainless construction, which is worth prioritizing if the grill will be permanently installed in an outdoor kitchen where repainting or replacing a grill head is expensive. Porcelain-coated grates, as found on the Tytus Fresno, retain heat well and resist rust but require more careful handling than bare stainless grates.

Burner count and configuration determine cooking flexibility more than total BTU numbers do. A 3-burner unit like the Hygrill STD gives you basic hot-and-cool zone control; the 4-burner Brand-Man and Monument Grills options add another zone for holding food warm. The Monument's 4+2 layout adds infrared side sear burners specifically for high-heat finishing, which is a meaningful distinction if steakhouse-style searing is a priority. A dedicated side burner — present on the Tytus, the Brand-Man 4-Burner Island, and the Monument — keeps sauces and sides off the main grate without requiring a separate outdoor burner. For a broader look at configurations suited to permanent installs, see Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas.

Fuel flexibility and installation type are the final filters. Every unit here runs on natural gas or can convert from propane, but the installation format varies sharply. Built-in drop-in heads — the Hygrill, both Brand-Man built-in options, and the Stanbroil griddle — require a countertop cutout and a fixed gas line, making them permanent fixtures suited to built-out kitchen islands. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Island and Tytus Fresno come as complete freestanding stations with storage cabinets and prep surfaces, which suits buyers who want a self-contained setup without masonry or tile work. If a freestanding island format fits your space better, the Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas page covers additional options in that format.

Frequently asked questions

What is the minimum budget for a quality natural gas heavy-duty outdoor grill?

Expect to spend at least $500–$800 for a heavy-duty natural gas grill with solid construction. The Monument Grills 4+2 Burner Cabinet Style comes in at $519 and represents the lower end of the range, while built-in stainless steel heads from Brand-Man start around $889.99. Purpose-built outdoor kitchen islands with storage and side burners — like the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner — run closer to $1,399.99. Below $500, BTU output, grate quality, and frame durability tend to drop off noticeably for natural gas applications.

Do I need a special conversion kit to run a propane grill on natural gas?

Most grills listed as "LPG/NG convertible" ship with the necessary orifices or include a conversion kit. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island and the Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head are both explicitly listed as natural gas convertible, meaning the hardware to switch fuels is either included or available from the manufacturer. Always confirm with the manufacturer before purchasing, because running a propane-tuned orifice on natural gas produces a weak, inefficient flame and can void the warranty.

Built-in grill head vs. freestanding island — which is right for an outdoor kitchen?

A built-in grill head, such as the Hygrill STD Series 3-Burner or the Stanbroil 28-Inch Natural Gas Griddle, drops into a custom masonry or stainless steel surround that you supply. That gives you full control over counter space and cabinet layout. A pre-built island like the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Station or the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island includes storage cabinets, prep surfaces, and side burners out of the box, so the total cost is lower if you don't already have outdoor kitchen infrastructure. For a deeper look at built-in configurations, see Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026).

How many BTUs do I actually need for a natural gas grill?

For most residential cooking — searing steaks, grilling chicken, smoking low and slow — a main burner output between 40,000 and 50,000 BTU across all burners is more than adequate. The Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner Built-In puts out 40,000 BTU; the Tytus Fresno delivers 48,000 BTU main plus a 12,000 BTU side burner. Raw BTU numbers mean little without also considering cooking area and grate material. Higher BTU with a smaller cooking surface can mean hot spots rather than better results. Focus on BTU-per-square-inch alongside total output.

Is 304 stainless steel worth the premium over standard stainless steel?

Yes, for any grill exposed to the elements year-round. Grade 304 stainless steel contains higher nickel and chromium content, which resists rust and pitting far better than the 430-grade stainless used in budget grills. Both the Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head and the Stanbroil 28-Inch Natural Gas Griddle specify 304 stainless steel construction. If you're installing a grill in a permanent outdoor kitchen, the extra corrosion resistance directly translates to a longer service life without refinishing or replacement.

What should I check before buying a natural gas grill for a permanent outdoor installation?

Confirm that your local gas line delivers sufficient pressure and flow rate for the grill's BTU demand — this typically requires a licensed plumber or gas fitter to verify. Check whether the grill ships as natural gas ready or requires a conversion kit, and whether that kit is included. For built-in heads, measure the cutout dimensions carefully; the Hygrill STD 3-Burner and Stanbroil 28-Inch Griddle have specific drop-in requirements. Finally, review local fire code setbacks for gas appliances, as clearances from combustible materials vary by jurisdiction. For a broader overview of compatible options, visit Natural Gas Outdoor Grills — Best Picks, Reviews & Guides.

The three-burner configuration is the main concession here. The lack of side burner functionality built into the head itself also means you'll need to plan surrounding cabinetry accordingly if sauce work or sautéing is part of the routine.

Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head, 30-Inch 4-Burner Propane Grill, Natural Gas Convertible, Heavy Duty 304 Stainless Steel 40,000BTUs BBQ Island Outdoor Kitchen

This Brand-Man built-in head hits a strong value point at $889.99 for a 304 stainless steel construction with 40,000 BTUs spread across four burners. The natural gas convertibility makes it future-proof — buy it for propane now, switch to a natural gas line later without replacing the unit. With 72 reviews at a perfect 5/5, it has a larger and equally satisfied user base than most built-in heads in this category. It's the right call for someone building an island on a budget without wanting to sacrifice material quality.

At 30 inches, the cooking surface is adequate for a family but tighter than the 44-inch or larger formats available elsewhere. Buyers planning to entertain regularly or cook multiple proteins simultaneously may feel constrained. Additionally, while 304 stainless is a genuine quality marker, the absence of extra features — side burners, storage, prep space — means this is a pure grill head, and the surrounding island infrastructure has to be sourced and built separately.

Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island with Side Burner - 44,000 BTU Stainless Steel BBQ Grill Station with Storage Cabinets, Fold-out Prep Table, LPG/NG Convertible, Black

For buyers who want a complete outdoor cooking station without the complexity of custom cabinetry, this Brand-Man island package delivers the most out-of-the-box functionality on the list at the lowest price point: $799.99. The 44,000 BTU output across four burners is the highest among the built-in-style options here, and the inclusion of storage cabinets, a fold-out prep table, and a side burner means this unit genuinely replaces a multi-piece outdoor kitchen setup. Its 4.7/5 from 113 reviews — the largest review pool of any product here — gives strong confidence in real-world performance. LPG/NG convertibility adds flexibility. Browse the broader Natural Gas Outdoor Grills hub if you're still comparing formats.

The black finish and station-style design won't appeal to everyone, particularly those integrating the grill into a masonry or tile island where stainless or a custom color is expected. Being a self-contained island rather than a true drop-in head also limits installation flexibility — it sits on its own frame and isn't designed to be cut into countertop surrounds. The 4.7 rating, while excellent, does reflect a small number of users who encountered assembly or fit-and-finish issues.

Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Propane or Natural Gas Island Grill – 864 sq. in. Cooking, 48,000 BTU Main + 12,000 BTU Side Burner, Porcelain Grates, Storage Cabinets, Semi-Portable - GM/Ash Stone

The Tytus Fresno is the most capable all-in-one unit on this list. At 864 square inches of cooking area, 48,000 BTU main output, a 12,000 BTU dedicated side burner, porcelain grates, and built-in storage cabinets, it covers every functional base a serious outdoor cook needs. The semi-portable design is a genuine differentiator — it can be repositioned as patio layouts change, unlike a fully cemented-in built-in. Propane and natural gas compatibility is included. At $1,399.99 it's the most expensive option here, but the feature set per dollar is defensible. This grill is explored further in the Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas (2026) guide.

With only 16 reviews at 4.2/5, the Tytus Fresno has the thinnest and lowest-rated feedback on the list. That's not disqualifying, but it does mean there's less collective real-world data to draw from compared to options with 70+ reviews. The ash stone exterior gives it a distinctive look that some will love and others will find doesn't blend with standard patio furniture or stainless appliances. Buyers prioritizing proven reliability over maximum feature count may want to weigh that review depth carefully before committing at this price.

Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle for Outdoor Kitchen Grill, Durable Stainless Steel 2-Burner Flat Top Grill with Lid for BBQ Island, 34,000 BTUs

The Stanbroil flat top griddle fills a specific gap: it's the only true griddle on this list, making it the right pick for anyone who wants to cook smash burgers, breakfast foods, stir-fries, or other flat-surface cooking in a permanent outdoor kitchen setup. At 34,000 BTUs across two burners and stainless steel construction, it's built to last in an outdoor environment. The $1,109.99 price and 4.5/5 from 20 reviews reflects a specialized, quality-focused product rather than a mass-market item. If flat top cooking is the priority — or if you're building an island with both a grill and a griddle station — this is the clear choice. See also the Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas (2026) guide for grill-only alternatives.

It's a griddle, not a grill — there are no grates, no direct flame cooking, and no sear marks. Buyers expecting traditional BBQ functionality will need to pair it with a conventional grill head in the same island. At 28 inches it's also on the smaller side for the price, and the two-burner format means heat zone control is limited compared to wider flat tops with more independent zones. The 20-review sample, while positive, is still relatively small for confident long-term durability assessment.

Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner Propane Gas Grills Stainless Steel Cabinet Style with Clearview® Lid, Knob Controls, Built-In Thermometer, Infrared Side Sear Burners & Side Burners, Blue

At $519, the Monument Grills cabinet-style unit is the most accessible price point on this list while still delivering a legitimate feature set: four main burners plus two infrared side sear burners, two additional side burners, a Clearview® tempered glass lid, and a built-in thermometer. The 5/5 from 71 reviews is an unusually strong consensus for a budget-adjacent grill and speaks to solid build quality relative to expectations. It's the right fit for someone who wants a freestanding, feature-rich natural gas grill without committing to a permanent outdoor kitchen installation. The blue finish is distinctive and will appeal to buyers who want something that stands out visually. Check the Best Grills For Natural Gas (2026) guide for a wider freestanding comparison.

The propane-primary labeling means buyers should confirm natural gas conversion kit availability and compatibility before purchasing if piped gas is the plan. The cabinet-style freestanding design also means it's not a built-in solution — it won't integrate flush into a masonry island. Despite the infrared sear burners being a genuine plus, the overall BTU output and cooking surface specs aren't listed in the available data, so buyers should verify those figures against their cooking volume needs before finalizing this choice over a purpose-built natural gas unit.

Buying guide

BTU output is the most cited spec in this category, but raw numbers only matter in context. A 3-burner built-in like the Hygrill STD Series covers its cooking area adequately without a stated BTU figure, while the Tytus Fresno delivers 48,000 BTU across five burners plus a dedicated 12,000 BTU side burner — roughly 10,000 BTU per zone, which is a reasonable benchmark for heavy searing. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Island puts out 44,000 BTU total, and the Brand-Man Built-In Head hits 40,000 BTU across four burners. Below about 9,000 BTU per main burner, recovery time after opening the lid suffers noticeably, so count burners and divide before assuming any number is impressive.

Cooking surface and configuration split this lineup into two categories: built-in grill heads and freestanding island stations. Built-in heads — the Hygrill STD, Brand-Man 30-Inch, and Stanbroil 28-Inch Griddle — drop into an existing outdoor kitchen frame and offer cleaner integration, but you supply the cabinetry. Freestanding island stations like the Tytus Fresno (864 sq. in. of cooking space, storage cabinets, fold-out prep table) and the Brand-Man Island (storage cabinets, fold-out prep table) arrive as complete workstations. If an outdoor kitchen build is already planned, a built-in head makes sense; if not, an island station covers prep, cooking, and storage without additional construction. For a deeper look at how these units fit into a full outdoor kitchen setup, see Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026).

Material grade determines long-term durability more than any other spec. Look for 304 stainless steel specifically — the Brand-Man Built-In Head calls it out explicitly, and it resists corrosion far better than the 430-grade steel common on budget grills. Grate material matters too: porcelain-coated cast iron, as used on the Tytus Fresno, retains heat well and releases food cleanly, but chips over time if mishandled. Flat-top griddle surfaces like the Stanbroil 28-Inch add versatility for smash burgers, fish, and vegetables that fall through standard grates, but they require more active maintenance to keep seasoned and rust-free.

Fuel flexibility and convertibility are worth checking before buying. Several units here — the Brand-Man Built-In Head, Brand-Man Island, and Tytus Fresno — are explicitly listed as natural gas/propane convertible, which protects the investment if your fuel source changes. The Monument Grills 4+2 Burner is propane-native with infrared side sear burners and a clearview lid, making it the most feature-dense freestanding option at $519, though it lacks the built-in or island form factor the other models offer. If you're building around a permanent natural gas line, confirm the unit ships with an NG orifice or conversion kit rather than requiring a separate purchase. More natural gas options across price points are covered in the main Natural Gas Outdoor Grills guide.

Related guides

If you're building a full outdoor kitchen setup, the guide to best gas grills for outdoor kitchen covers built-in heads and island-ready options in detail. For a broader look at the category, the best barbeque grills natural gas guide compares top-rated BBQ-focused natural gas models across different budgets and configurations.

Frequently asked questions

What is the minimum price for a quality heavy-duty natural gas grill?

Expect to spend at least $500–$800 for a grill that combines solid BTU output, 304 stainless steel construction, and natural gas compatibility. The Monument Grills 4+2 Burner model comes in at $519 and represents the lower end of the heavy-duty range, while built-in grill heads and island-style units from brands like Brand-Man and Hygrill start closer to $890–$1,130. Full island stations with storage cabinets and side burners, such as the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner, reach $1,400. Budget options under $500 typically sacrifice BTU output, burner quality, or steel gauge.

What is the difference between a built-in grill head and a freestanding island grill?

A built-in grill head — like the Hygrill STD Series 3-Burner or the Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner head — is designed to drop into a custom outdoor kitchen frame or BBQ island you already own or are having built. It ships without a cabinet base. A freestanding island grill, such as the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island with Side Burner or the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island Grill, includes the full enclosure, storage cabinets, and prep surfaces. If you are building an outdoor kitchen from scratch and want to spec the cabinet separately, a grill head gives you more flexibility. If you want a complete, ready-to-use setup, go with an island station.

Can propane grills be converted to natural gas?

Some models are factory-rated as convertible, meaning the manufacturer supplies or sells a conversion kit designed specifically for that unit. The Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner Built-In Head is listed as natural gas convertible, and the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island is labeled LPG/NG Convertible. The Tytus Fresno is sold as a propane or natural gas unit. Attempting a DIY conversion on a grill not rated for it is a safety risk and typically voids the warranty. Always verify that the specific model you purchase is approved for conversion before buying a conversion kit separately. For more options, see the full best grills for natural gas roundup.

How do Hygrill and Brand-Man compare for built-in grill heads?

Both target the outdoor kitchen built-in segment with 304 stainless steel construction and natural gas support. The Hygrill STD Series is a 3-burner unit priced at $1,129.99 with a perfect 5/5 rating across 41 reviews. The Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner head costs $889.99, also rates 5/5, and carries more reviews at 72 — it also adds the option for natural gas conversion from propane and advertises 40,000 BTUs. If you need a fourth burner and want to save roughly $240, the Brand-Man head is the stronger value. If your build calls for a 3-burner footprint or you prefer the Hygrill's fit and finish, that price premium may be justified.

Is a flat-top griddle a practical alternative to a traditional grill grate for natural gas outdoor cooking?

Yes, for certain cooking styles. The Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle delivers 34,000 BTUs across a flat stainless steel surface and installs into an outdoor kitchen island just like a grill head. It suits smash burgers, breakfast items, seafood, and vegetables that would fall through standard grates. The trade-off is the lack of grill marks and open-flame char. Many outdoor kitchen builds now combine a traditional grill head with a separate griddle insert — the Stanbroil at $1,109.99 is priced comparably to premium grill heads, making this a realistic addition rather than a compromise. Browse the broader natural gas BBQ grill category for side-by-side comparisons.

What cooking area and BTU output should buyers look for in a heavy-duty natural gas grill?

For a family of four to six, a primary cooking surface of 600–900 square inches paired with 40,000–50,000 BTUs across the main burners is a practical benchmark. The Tytus Fresno covers 864 sq. in. with 48,000 BTUs on the main burners plus a 12,000 BTU side burner — one of the highest-output options in this category. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Island reaches 44,000 BTUs with a full cabinet station. BTU numbers alone do not determine heat quality; burner spacing, grate material, and lid seal all affect real-world performance. Porcelain-coated cast iron grates, as found on the Tytus Fresno, retain heat better than plain steel grates. See the full outdoor grills natural gas guide for a deeper breakdown.

Disclosure: This page contains affiliate links. We may earn a commission if you purchase through them — at no extra cost to you. Recommendations are based on independent testing and editorial judgment.

Natural gas outdoor grills cover everything from freestanding cabinet-style units to built-in grill heads and complete island stations designed for permanent outdoor kitchens. Shoppers here range from homeowners running a gas line to an existing patio to serious backyard cooks building a full outdoor kitchen with storage, side burners, and integrated griddles. The core decision dimensions are configuration (built-in head vs. freestanding vs. full island), BTU output and burner count, cooking surface area, and whether the unit ships ready for natural gas or requires a propane-to-NG conversion kit. Material quality — particularly 304 stainless steel construction and porcelain or stainless grates — separates entry-level picks from heavy-duty options built to handle daily use and weather exposure year-round. For buyers focused on specific use cases, see the dedicated guides on best gas grills for outdoor kitchens, best barbeque grills in natural gas, and the broader best outdoor grills natural gas roundup.

Subcategories

Built-In Grills for Outdoor Kitchens

This cluster covers grill heads and built-in units designed to drop into a stone, concrete, or steel island frame — the kind of permanent setup where aesthetics and BTU output both matter. Products like the Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built In Grill and the Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head fall squarely here, offering stainless steel construction and direct natural gas hookups that eliminate the need for propane tanks. It's the right category if you're building or upgrading a fixed outdoor kitchen rather than buying a freestanding cart grill. Explore the best gas grills for outdoor kitchens.

Freestanding and Island Natural Gas Grills

For buyers who want a complete, self-contained grilling station — cabinets, side burners, prep tables, and all — this subcategory focuses on full island and cart-style natural gas grills. Units like the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island Grill and the Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island with Side Burner combine high BTU output with built-in storage, making them practical for frequent entertainers who don't want to integrate into masonry. Most models in this group are LPG/NG convertible, so they work whether your yard is plumbed for natural gas or not. See the top-rated outdoor natural gas grills.

BBQ and Barbecue Natural Gas Grills

This group targets traditional barbecue-style cooking on natural gas — think multi-burner setups with porcelain or stainless grates, high-capacity cooking surfaces, and classic cabinet styling. It overlaps with freestanding grills but skews toward buyers who prioritize low-and-slow BBQ performance alongside searing ability, rather than integrated kitchen functionality. Browse the best natural gas barbeque grills or check the closely related barbecue grills natural gas guide for additional picks and comparisons.

Featured products

The Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built In Grill, Stainless Steel Grill Head for Outdoor Kitchen, Natural Gas earns a perfect 5/5 from 41 reviewers and is built specifically for drop-in outdoor kitchen installations, with a heavy-duty stainless steel head priced at $1,129.99. It's a strong pick for homeowners who want a clean, integrated look without sacrificing build quality.

The Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head, 30-Inch 4-Burner Propane Grill, Natural Gas Convertible, Heavy Duty 304 Stainless Steel 40,000BTUs BBQ Island Outdoor Kitchen delivers 40,000 BTUs across four burners with 304-grade stainless steel construction, and at $889.99 it holds a perfect 5/5 rating across 72 reviews — the highest review count among the built-in heads here. Its natural gas convertibility adds long-term flexibility.

For buyers who want a complete outdoor cooking station rather than a grill head alone, the Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island with Side Burner - 44,000 BTU Stainless Steel BBQ Grill Station with Storage Cabinets, Fold-out Prep Table, LPG/NG Convertible, Black bundles 44,000 BTUs, a side burner, fold-out prep table, and enclosed storage cabinets into a single unit at $799.99, backed by 113 reviews averaging 4.7/5.

The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Propane or Natural Gas Island Grill is the highest-output option in this group, combining a 48,000 BTU main burner array with a 12,000 BTU side burner and 864 sq. in. of cooking space across porcelain grates — all in a semi-portable island format with storage cabinets at $1,399.99. It suits large households or anyone who regularly cooks for a crowd. For more complete outdoor kitchen setups, see the Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen guide.

The Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle for Outdoor Kitchen Grill, Durable Stainless Steel 2-Burner Flat Top Grill with Lid for BBQ Island, 34,000 BTUs fills a specific niche: a flat-top griddle surface rather than grate cooking, making it ideal for breakfast items, smash burgers, or seafood. Its 34,000 BTUs across two burners and lidded design are uncommon in built-in natural gas griddles at this $1,109.99 price point.

The Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner Propane Gas Grills Stainless Steel Cabinet Style with Clearview® Lid, Knob Controls, Built-In Thermometer, Infrared Side Sear Burners & Side Burners, Blue is the most affordable option at $519 and packs infrared side sear burners alongside two additional side burners — a feature set that typically appears on grills costing considerably more. Its Clearview® lid and 5/5 rating from 71 buyers make it a compelling freestanding alternative. Browse additional options in the Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas and Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas roundups.

Common questions in this category

Is a natural gas grill actually worth it over propane?

For anyone who cooks outdoors regularly, a direct natural gas connection eliminates the hassle of monitoring and swapping propane tanks — fuel is always on and the cost per BTU is typically lower than propane over time. The trade-off is that the grill must sit within reach of a gas line, so placement is fixed rather than flexible. If your outdoor space is permanent and you grill more than a few times a month, the convenience and long-term savings usually justify the conversion or upfront purchase.

See the top-rated natural gas outdoor grills →

What should you look for when buying a built-in grill for an outdoor kitchen?

Built-in grills need to fit a specific cabinet cutout, hold up to weather without a cover-friendly design, and be made from marine-grade or 304 stainless steel so they don't rust flush against surrounding stonework or cabinetry. BTU output, cooking area, and whether the unit ships ready for natural gas (or requires a conversion kit) are the other critical specs to confirm before ordering. A grill head rated for outdoor kitchen installation is not interchangeable with a freestanding unit — the ventilation, trim, and mounting requirements differ substantially.

Explore the best gas grills for outdoor kitchens →

How many BTUs do you actually need for outdoor grilling?

BTU ratings are useful for comparison but misleading in isolation — a well-designed 40,000 BTU grill with good heat retention will outperform a poorly built 60,000 BTU model. A practical rule of thumb is 80–100 BTUs per square inch of primary cooking area, which means a 400 sq. in. grill needs roughly 32,000–40,000 BTUs to sear and recover temperature quickly. Side burners, infrared sear zones, and griddle surfaces each carry separate BTU specs and should be evaluated independently from the main burner output.

Browse high-BTU natural gas barbecue grills →
